title: Chapter 13: The Counter-Reformation
content: <!-- Speed: Sprint | D4+D1 -->
By noon, the hashtag #CancelQueenArtemis had 50,000 posts. By 2 PM, it had 120,000. The algorithm, as algorithms do, had smelled blood and sent its sharks.

Sam had been watching the numbers for three hours. Her iced coffee was warm. Her spreadsheet had grown three new columns: "Negative Comments,""Positive Comments,""Comments That Are Just GIFs of People Eating Popcorn."
"The popcorn column is winning," Sam reported.

Chloe was on the bench, doing a consultation for a woman named Priya who had driven from San Jose. Priya was a product manager who had been told she was "too aggressive" in meetings and had internalized this until she'd stopped speaking entirely. Chloe was explaining that "too aggressive" was code for "correct while female," and Priya was crying in the good way.
"Should I postpone the consultations?" Chloe asked, glancing at Sam's laptop.
"No. The people who actually show up are the real ones. The people posting popcorn GIFs were never going to show up anyway."
The first counter-video went up at 3:17 PM.

It was from an account called @strawberrygirl.recovery. The video showed a young woman — the same woman who had been holding a strawberry at arm's length against the pink windmill sky — sitting in her kitchen. She wasn't wearing beige. She was wearing pajamas.

"Hi," Strawberry Girl said. "My name is Jess. A week ago, I was at the Strawberry Dream Farm trying to take a photo that would make my ex-boyfriend regret everything. A woman in a plastic crown showed up and told me the strawberry couldn't give me the validation I wanted, and that I should call my mom. I thought she was crazy. But I called my mom anyway. We talked for three hours. I told her things I've never told anyone."
Jess paused. Her eyes were wet but her voice was steady.

"That woman — Chloe, Queen Artemis, whatever you want to call her — didn't charge me anything. She didn't try to sell me a candle. She just told me the truth. And now people are calling her a bully?" Jess laughed, the kind of laugh that had some anger underneath it. "She's not a bully. She's the first person in years who told me something I actually needed to hear. Leave her alone."
The video had 200,000 views within an hour.
Sam refreshed her laptop. "Jess from the strawberry farm just entered the chat."
"The liberated return," Chloe said. "Classic quest narrative. The people you save come back to save you."
"That's a thing?"
"It's the most important thing."
More videos came.

Marcus, the San Diego engineer, posted from his car — he'd quit his job. He was parked at a rest stop somewhere in Arizona. "I met this woman yesterday. She told me I'd min-maxed the wrong stats. I'm driving to Colorado to play D&D with my college friends for the first time in ten years. This is the best decision I've ever made. Also, I canceled my Peloton subscription." 400,000 views.

The trucker from the Desert Rose Diner posted a thirty-second clip from his cab. "I met the crown lady at a diner outside Bakersfield. She wasn't selling anything. Just eating pancakes and telling the truth. All these people calling her a bully — you know what a bully is? A bully is someone who charges five thousand dollars to teach you how to breathe. She does it for free." 350,000 views.

Bucket Hat Guy — whose name turned out to be Tyler — posted a video holding his bucket hat. "I bought this for forty-two dollars because an influencer wore one. Chloe told me it was a forty-dollar insecurity. She was right. I donated the hat. I bought a beanie. It was nine dollars. I like it more." 600,000 views.

Sam's spreadsheet was updating faster than she could type. Column L: "Liberation Receipts (Views)." Column M: "Public Opinion Shift (1-10)." Column M had gone from 2 to 7 in the space of an afternoon.
"The counter-reformation is working," Sam said.
"The people have spoken," Chloe said.
"The people have spoken, and the people said: 'Actually, the crazy lady with the plastic crown was right about everything.'"
"That's usually how it goes in the third act."

By sunset, Celeste had taken down her video. The #CancelQueenArtemis hashtag had been replaced by #WeStanAGirlInACrown, which had 2 million posts and counting. Someone had started a GoFundMe for Chloe's burrito budget that had raised $4,000 in two hours. Sam had redirected it to a mutual aid fund for service workers in Santa Monica because, as she put it, "we're already funded by the universe, and the universe apparently has excellent PR."

Chloe sat on the pier bench, watching the sunset do its screensaver thing. She was tired — the good kind of tired, the kind you feel after a battle you didn't know you were going to fight but won anyway.
"Volume two is going well," she said to the empty space beside her.
Sam didn't look up from her laptop. "Are you talking to the readers again?"

"Yes. They deserve an update. Volume two: the kingdom faced its first organized opposition from a pretender queen and a hostile algorithm. The liberated returned to defend their liberator. Public opinion shifted 180 degrees in six hours. Strong narrative arc. Satisfying reversal."
Sam closed her laptop. "You know what I just realized?"
"What?"
"You spent all of Volume one liberating other people. Volume two is about other people liberating you."
Chloe considered this. The crown caught the last light of the sunset.
"That's not a Volume two thing," she said. "That's just what happens when you tell the truth to enough people. Eventually, they start telling it back."
